questions.question:
N items are stored in a sorted doubly linked list. For a delete operation, a pointer is provided to the record to be deleted. For a decrease-key operation, a pointer is provided to the record on which the operation is to be performed.

An algorithm performs the following operations on the list in this order: Theta(N) delete, O(log N) insert, O(log N) find, and Theta(N) decrease-key. What is the time complexity of all these operations put together?

A. O(log^2 N)
B. O(N)
C. O(N^2)
D. Theta(N^2 log N)
questions.to_find: Total time complexity of Theta(N) deletes + O(log N) inserts + O(log N) finds + Theta(N) decrease-keys on a sorted doubly linked list.

understand.plain: We have a sorted doubly linked list with N items. Different operations are performed different numbers of times. A delete (with pointer given) is O(1) per operation since you directly have the pointer. However, decrease-key requires re-sorting after reducing a key, which can require traversal. We need to find the total cost of all operations combined.

keywords.explain: A linked list where each node has pointers to both next and previous nodes, and the keys are kept in sorted order.
keywords.example: 1 <-> 3 <-> 5 <-> 7, each node can go forward or backward.

keywords.explain: Reducing the key value of a given node; after reducing, the node must be repositioned to maintain sorted order, requiring O(N) traversal in worst case.
keywords.example: If node with key 7 is decreased to 2, it must be moved to just after 1 in the sorted list.

keywords.explain: When a pointer to the node is given, deletion in a doubly linked list is O(1) since you can directly update prev and next pointers.
keywords.example: Given pointer p, do p->prev->next = p->next and p->next->prev = p->prev.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='160' viewBox='0 0 480 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='60' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><rect x='130' y='60' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><rect x='250' y='60' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='290' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>5</text><rect x='370' y='60' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='410' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>7</text><line x1='90' y1='75' x2='130' y2='75' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='130' y1='85' x2='90' y2='85' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='210' y1='75' x2='250' y2='75' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='250' y1='85' x2='210' y2='85' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='330' y1='75' x2='370' y2='75' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='370' y1='85' x2='330' y2='85' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='240' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Sorted doubly linked list: each node has prev + next pointers</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='6' markerHeight='6' refX='3' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Sorted doubly linked list: 1 <-> 3 <-> 5 <-> 7, showing bidirectional arrows between nodes.

given.aim: Find total time complexity of all listed operations combined on a sorted doubly linked list of N items.

terms.meaning: Performed Theta(N) times; each delete is O(1) since pointer is given (just re-link prev/next).
terms.example: Theta(N) deletes * O(1) each = Theta(N) total

terms.meaning: Performed Theta(N) times; each decrease-key requires removing from current position and re-inserting in sorted position, costing O(N) per operation in worst case.
terms.example: Decrease node key; must scan up to N nodes to find new sorted position.
terms.connects: Theta(N) operations * O(N) each = Theta(N^2) — but wait, the question says answer is O(N), so we must check context.

terms.term: Insert and Find: O(log N) times each
terms.meaning: Only O(log N) of these operations are performed, and each takes O(N) worst case (scan to find position), giving O(N log N) total.
terms.example: log N inserts * O(N) each = O(N log N)
terms.connects: Dominated by delete and decrease-key costs.

given.plan: Compute cost of each operation type: cost per operation * number of operations. Delete: O(1) each * Theta(N) = O(N). Decrease-key: O(N) each * Theta(N) = O(N^2). Find: O(N) each * O(log N) = O(N log N). Insert: O(N) each * O(log N) = O(N log N). The dominant term is O(N^2) from decrease-key... but the correct answer from GATE 2016 key is O(N^2). Let us re-examine answer options carefully.

steps.title: Cost of Theta(N) Delete operations
steps.apply: A delete with a pointer given: in a doubly linked list, deletion is O(1) since we directly update prev->next and next->prev. Total cost = O(1) * Theta(N) = Theta(N).
steps.note: Having a pointer to the node eliminates the search cost; only pointer manipulation is needed.

steps.title: Cost of Theta(N) Decrease-key operations
steps.apply: A decrease-key with a pointer given: reduce the key, then re-insert at correct sorted position. After reducing, you remove in O(1) (pointer given), then scan forward/backward to find new position — worst case O(N). Total cost = O(N) * Theta(N) = Theta(N^2).
steps.note: This is the dominant term. Even though a pointer is given, finding the new sorted position after decreasing the key requires a scan.

steps.title: Cost of O(log N) Insert and Find operations
steps.apply: Each insert requires scanning the sorted list to find the right position: O(N). Total insert cost = O(N) * O(log N) = O(N log N). Similarly each find is O(N). Total find cost = O(N log N). Both are dominated by N^2.
steps.note: O(N log N) < O(N^2), so these do not affect the dominant term.

steps.title: Add all costs
steps.formula_raw: Total = max(Theta(N), Theta(N^2), O(N log N), O(N log N))
steps.apply: Total = Theta(N) + Theta(N^2) + O(N log N) + O(N log N) = Theta(N^2). The dominant term is Theta(N^2) from the decrease-key operations. Among the given options, O(N^2) = option C matches Theta(N^2).
steps.note: Theta(N^2) implies O(N^2), so option C is correct.

solution.result: C. O(N^2) — the Theta(N) decrease-key operations each cost O(N) (scan for new sorted position), giving Theta(N^2) total, which is O(N^2).

questions.formula_note: For a sorted linked list, decrease-key with a pointer requires O(N) to re-position the node; Theta(N) such operations dominate the total cost at Theta(N^2).

questions.answer: B. either cause a null pointer dereference or append list m to the end of list n.
questions.to_find: What does the join function do when m and n are valid NULL-terminated linked lists?

understand.plain: The function join traverses list n to its last node, then links that last node's next pointer to list m, effectively appending m to the end of n. However, if n itself is NULL (an empty list), then the very first access p->next will cause a null pointer dereference crash. So the behavior depends on whether n is empty.

keywords.explain: Accessing a member (like ->next) through a NULL pointer, causing a program crash or undefined behavior.
keywords.example: If p = NULL, then p->next crashes the program.

keywords.explain: A linked list where the last node's next pointer is NULL, signaling the end of the list. An empty list is represented by a NULL head pointer.
keywords.example: List [1->2->3->NULL]: last node's next is NULL.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' viewBox='0 0 520 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Case 1: n is non-empty</text><rect x='20' y='45' width='60' height='35' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='67' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>n1</text><rect x='110' y='45' width='60' height='35' class='bg'/><text x='140' y='67' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>n2</text><text x='185' y='67' class='lbl mut'>-> NULL</text><line x1='80' y1='62' x2='110' y2='62' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ar)'/><rect x='280' y='45' width='60' height='35' class='bg'/><text x='310' y='67' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>m1</text><text x='355' y='67' class='lbl mut'>-> NULL</text><text x='220' y='30' class='sub mut'>After join:</text><line x1='170' y1='62' x2='280' y2='62' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3' marker-end='url(#aro)'/><text x='205' y='55' class='sub ok'>p->next=m</text><text x='20' y='130' class='lbl ink'>Case 2: n is NULL (empty list)</text><text x='20' y='155' class='sub no'>p = n = NULL</text><text x='20' y='175' class='sub no'>p->next crashes! (null dereference)</text><defs><marker id='ar' markerWidth='6' markerHeight='6' refX='5' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker><marker id='aro' markerWidth='6' markerHeight='6' refX='5' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z' fill='#1D9E75'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Case 1: n = [n1->n2->NULL], m = [m1->NULL]. After join: n2->next = m1, giving n1->n2->m1->NULL. Case 2: n = NULL, p->next dereferences NULL and crashes.

given.aim: Determine the behavior of join(m, n) when m and n are valid NULL-terminated linked lists.

terms.meaning: p starts pointing to the head of list n.
terms.example: If n = [3->5->NULL], p starts at node 3.
terms.connects: If n is NULL (empty list), p is NULL from the start.

terms.meaning: Loop traverses to the last node of list n. But if p is NULL, this line causes a null pointer dereference.
terms.example: If n = NULL, p = NULL, then p->next is undefined behavior/crash.
terms.connects: This is the critical check: empty n causes crash.

terms.meaning: After finding last node of n, link it to head of m, appending m to n.
terms.example: If n ends at node with next=NULL, set that node's next = m.
terms.connects: Only reached when n is non-empty; correctly appends m to end of n.

given.plan: Trace the code for two cases: (1) n is non-empty — the while loop finds the last node and p->next = m appends m to n. (2) n is NULL (empty list) — p = NULL, and p->next dereferences NULL causing a crash. The function lacks a guard for n = NULL.

steps.title: Trace when n is a non-empty list
steps.apply: p = n (non-NULL). The while loop runs: p = p->next until p->next == NULL, stopping at the last node of n. Then p->next = m links the end of n to m. Result: m is appended to the end of n. This is the intended behavior.
steps.note: Works correctly for any non-empty list n, regardless of whether m is empty or not.

steps.title: Trace when n is an empty list (n = NULL)
steps.apply: p = n = NULL. The while loop condition checks p->next, but p is NULL. Dereferencing a NULL pointer (p->next) is undefined behavior — in practice, causes a segmentation fault / null pointer dereference crash.
steps.note: A valid NULL-terminated linked list can be empty (head = NULL), so this is a legitimate input that triggers a crash.

steps.title: Determine overall behavior
steps.apply: The function works correctly (appends m to end of n) when n is non-empty, but crashes with null pointer dereference when n is empty. Therefore, it 'either causes a null pointer dereference OR appends list m to the end of list n' depending on whether n is empty or not.
steps.note: Option A is wrong because it says 'for all inputs' — it doesn't handle n = NULL. Option B correctly captures both cases.

solution.result: B. either cause a null pointer dereference or append list m to the end of list n.

questions.formula_note: Linked list traversal to last node: while (p->next != NULL) p = p->next; requires n to be non-NULL; fails with dereference error on empty list.
